---
title: "The Cure in Cardiff"
description: "Blackweir Fields, June 24."
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2026-07-01"
image: ../../images/the cure cover.jpg
categories:
  - Live Music
  - Review
---

## The Cure at Blackweir Fields, Cardiff

![](../../images/the cure.jpg)

*The Cure at Blackweir Fields*

A twelve-hour round trip truly worth the exhaustion. I’ve known and loved The Cure for a very long time. There are many a childhood drive cemented in my memory with ‘Pictures of You’ playing in the background. It was only in my teenage years that I truly became a fan, largely through some of my favourite films. In Starter for Ten (2006), the comedy starring James McAvoy as Brian Jackson, a working-class university student who fulfils his lifelong ambition to compete in University Challenge, Brian’s shot of leaving home is soundtracked by ‘Boys Don’t Cry’. I first heard ‘Inbetween Days’ in Sing Street (2016), a brilliant film about a group of schoolboys who form a band in 1980s Dublin. The band constantly reinvent themselves, trying to find their own unique sound, defining their Cure era as the epitome of ‘happy sad’. I love this phrase, happy sad. I think it encapsulates so well many things in life that cannot fall easily into positive or negative emotion. It is the inevitable melancholy that accompanies major life decisions or unexpected twists. And it’s true, if The Cure are truly great at anything, its capturing what it means to be happy sad. 

I jumped at the opportunity to see them live. After crossing the Prince of Wales bridge, we were immediately confronted by a never-ending queue. Eventually, after much frustration, we crawled into Cardiff, only to find half the roads in the city closed for cars, including the only entrance to our pre-paid parking. Sad. After a few clueless loops of the one-way system, we eventually found street parking, and prepared to take the financial hit and go, were pleasantly surprised that we were outside tariff times. Happy. A longer walk in the scorching sun but we made it to Blackweir fields eventually. Happy sad. 

There were a few things that struck me as incredibly special about this concert. As I was standing in the packed crowd, watching people who were born decades after these songs were released crying with emotion at Robert Smith’s timeless voice, it was clear that The Cure have something rare: longevity. It was truly an intergenerational concert. I went with my dad, and families with adult children were dime a dozen. This is a band that has been shared through childhood, bonded over, soundtracked lives across generations. It’s a testament to the emotional pull and quality of the music. The set was a whopping two and a half hours (and my feet certainly felt it), but every song was worth the wait. 

Onto my second point, and something that lends credibility to Smith’s longevity, is the brilliance of his voice. This is a sore spot for many singers, the inevitable decline in vocal quality as artists get older. But Smith’s voice was beautiful as ever; he sounded fantastic. I think it helps that he’s still writing and releasing music, there is no staleness here, certainly a legacy but no legacy act. 

But it’s not just Smith’ s vocals that get their time to shine. As well as a long setlist, one of the best parts about this gig was the gorgeously long intros. I mean, it’s what The Cure are known for, what else would you expect? The iconic intro of ‘Pictures of You’ was cut a little, if only to make space for the numerous instrumental artistries in hits like ‘The Last Day of Summer’, ‘A Forest’, ‘A Night’, ‘The Walk’ etc. It took me a moment to realise why this stayed with me so. I think that The Cure and their popularity attests to an openness to reimagine an industry that still allows for four-minute instrumental intros in mainstream music. I think it is something that is declining in a lot of popular music, bypassed in favour of creating catchy hooks and instant gratification that can succeed in contemporary music metrics. People are far more impatient in a world of constant scrolling. A song may blow up on TikTok, but it will do so for a few bars of perfect sound clip splendour, rather than a fully crafted song. The Cure is so refreshing because you know you are listening to a world in a song, there is patience there, texture, landscape. You will be satisfied, just perhaps not straight away. There is a certain nostalgia to this, and a gravitas that is earned. For many up-and-coming artists, these approaches would be a risk. There are many brilliant experimental artists, but they’re not selling out 30,000 tickets. The Cure can be patient, they can perform at their own pace, because they’ve already cemented the pull of their music. I think this sort of patience would be a goldmine in the modern music industry. 

Overall, this was a brilliant gig. The standout performances for me were of course ‘Pictures of You’, ‘Boys Don’t Cry’ and ‘Inbetween Days’, as well as a short interlude on a little penny whistle, and a truly brilliant rendition of ‘A Forest’. Even the thirty minute trudge back to the car and hours driving through an obstacle course of nocturnal animals couldn’t dampen the brilliance. It was the highest temperatures in June on record (so far) and this gig was still the hottest thing around.
